data.title: U.S. investment in Intel a 'terrible decision bad for almost everyone' - Cato Institute
data.description:
President Donald Trump's administration on Friday confirmed a massive $11.1B investment in beleaguered U.S. legacy chipmaker Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), a move that was called a "terrible decision bad for almost everyone" by the Cato Institute.

Intel called the agreement "historic" and said it was "deeply committed" to ensuring the world’s most advanced technologies were "American made."

Founded in 1977 and based in Washington, D.C., the Cato Institute is a think tank specializing in public policy. Scott Lincicome, vice president of general economics at Cato’s Herbert A. Stiefel Center for Trade Policy Studies, outlined six reasons for his view. 

"Overall, it's a horrendous move that will have real harms for U.S. companies, U.S. tech leadership, and the U.S. economy overall. Hopefully, they don't go through with it," he said on Friday on X (formerly Twitter).

According to Lincicome, the move would result in Intel's (NASDAQ:INTC) decisions being increasingly driven by politics, not commercial considerations, thus harming its long-term viability. State-owned enterprises "are notoriously slow, bloated, & unproductive," he said. 

Intel's (INTC) competitors, meanwhile, would now have to compete against it and "Uncle Sam for customers, capital, etc."

"Bad for Intel’s customers who now must fear (and may face pressure to) they'll be pressured/forced to buy Intel’s products, regardless of their merit. (Intel is STILL struggling to make a top-end chip.) This is in turn bad for the U.S. tech sector overall," Lincicome said. 

"Bad for U.S. companies that took (government) subsidies (CHIPs/IRA/etc): Are they next? Should they return the money? Will they preempt via political moves/lobbying? (Buyer beware, sure, but they didn’t sign up for this, and their investments may be distorted further)," he said.

"Bad for companies/industries plausibly linked to 'national security' – not only might Uncle Sam pressure them, but investors may think twice (about) them and instead look elsewhere for less-risky targets (here or abroad)," the policy researcher added. 

Finally, the U.S. government's investment in Intel (INTC) bodes ill for U.S. exports. Now that the legacy chipmaker would be a state-owned enterprise, its sales could be deemed as "government subsidies" by foreign administrations and could be thus targeted with "countervailing duties."

"There are DOZENS of ways the U.S. (government) could support Intel - market and non-market support - that don't involve taking a massive shareholding stake in the flailing company. Subsidies, l/t contracts, visas, tariff relief, permitting relief, tax relief, etc etc. All better," Lincicome said. 

Shares of Intel (INTC) advanced +5.5% on Friday after the investment confirmation and amid a broader market rally. The U.S. investment marks a notable turnaround from just a few weeks ago when Trump called on the company's top boss, Lip-Bu Tan, to resign over alleged ties to China.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Why Costco’s $1.50 hot dog deal has become a retail icon
data.description:
For more than four decades, the $1.50 hot dog has been a symbol of Costco Wholesale's (NASDAQ:COST) efforts to separate itself from the retail pack as a customer-friendly business.

Due to inflation, the $1.50 hot dog cuts into Costco's (NASDAQ:COST) profits on a nominal basis but is widely considered an overall driver of store traffic and membership loyalty.

"The hotdog became a psychological anchor, a reminder to customers that Costco is built on trust, fairness, and value that feels untouchable. Even though the company loses close to $300M annually on the deal, it wins far more in membership renewals, loyalty, and priceless brand equity," highlighted Shopper AI brand strategist Alon Barak.

The history of the $1.50 hot dog at Costco (COSTCO) tracks to the introduction at the food court in 1984, shortly after the company’s 1983 founding. In a short time, the hot dog and soda combo quickly became a defining feature of the Costco (COST) shopping experience. As membership and food courts expanded, Costco (COST) transitioned to manufacturing its own Kirkland-branded hot dogs. The company also opened facilities in California and Illinois to reduce supply chain costs and preserve the low price.

Costco (COST) management has long been vocal about the significance of this offering. When former CEO Craig Jelinek broached the idea of raising the price due to losses, co-founder Jim Sinegal famously replied, "If you raise the effing hot dog, I will kill you. Figure it out." More recently, executives including long-time CFO Richard Galanti and current CEO Ron Vachris have publicly declared their commitment to maintaining the $1.50 price.

During Costco's (COST) FQ3 earnings conference call, Chief Financial Officer Gary Millerchip highlighted once again that the retail giant was holding the line on the price for its iconic food court menu item. 

Sam's Club (WMT) has made a strong attempt to cut into Costco's (COST) well-known deal by offering a similar hot dog and soda combo at its food court. Sam’s Club combo is priced at $1.38, making it actually less expensive than Costco's (COST) $1.50 option. Notably, both combos feature a quarter-pound all-beef hot dog served with a fountain drink, but Sam’s Club gives customers a larger soda size.

Costco (COST) food courts began switching their fountain soda supply from PepsiCo (PEP) back to Coca-Cola (KO) products in the summer of 2025, while Sam’s Club offers PepsiCo products (PEP) at its food court.

data.title: Reaction Roundup: Experts weigh in on Fed chair Powell's Jackson Hole comments
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday notched its best day since late May, sparked by Federal Reserve interest rate cut expectations. 

Fed chair Jerome Powell at the Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium did not fully commit to cutting rates but did note that the downside risks to the labor market were increasing and that the "baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance."

Following a significantly weaker-than-expected July nonfarm payrolls report earlier this month, anticipation was high going into Powell's speech that the central bank chief would provide some signals about easing monetary policy. 

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) on Friday advanced +1.52% for its best day since May 27 and ended just shy of its record close. Meanwhile, the market rally helped the blue-chip Dow (DJI) finally post a new record close for the first time since December.

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the S&P and the Dow: (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), (NYSEARCA:SPXU), (NYSEARCA:DIA), (NYSEARCA:DDM), (NYSEARCA:UDOW), (NYSEARCA:DOG), (NYSEARCA:DXD), and (NYSEARCA:SDOW) 

See below for various reactions to Powell's speech at Jackson Hole:

Joseph Brusuelas, principal and chief economist at RSM US LLP:

"The Kansas City Fed’s Jackson Hole Monetary Policy Symposium presented Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell with an occasion to give investors and policymakers the words that they wanted to hear on a possible September rate.  Powell more than delivered by noting 'the baseline outlook and shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting out policy stance' which opens the door to one or more near term rate cuts pending labor market and inflation data.

The Powell pivot towards a possible rate cut is now predicated on the August jobs report that will be published on September 5, 2025 arriving weak or somewhere below 50,000 given what appears to be a new breakeven rate that Powell pointed to in his statement."

Neil Dutta, head of economics at Renaissance Macro Research:

"This is about the September Fed meeting. If you get solid economic data, you’ll get a hawkish cut in September. If you get bad data, you’ll get a dovish cut. 

That’s how to think about this. Through the lens of an insurance cut being green lit in September. Powell did not lay the ground work for a series of rate cuts. 

I think two remains a reasonable baseline for this year but given my economic outlook, the market might be underestimating the number of cuts in 2026."

Dario Perkins, managing director of global macro at GlobalData.TSLombard:

"Surprising to see Powell offer such a clear policy signal with so much data before the next FOMC meeting (and yes, was a clear signal)."

David Rosenberg, founder and president of Rosenberg Research & Associates:

"With the Fed staff lowering inflation projections, muted tariff impacts, and the possibility of unemployment rising above the natural rate, we may see significant slack in the labor market extending through 2027.

Reading between the lines, this was not just a nod toward a September cut — but a signal that a sequence of rate cuts could follow."

Michael Feroli, chief U.S. economist at JPMorgan:

"Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks confirmed market and consensus expectations for the FOMC to cut the funds rate at its next meeting in mid-September."

Felix-Antoine Vezina-Poirier, strategist for Daily Insights at BCA Research:

"Powell's JH speech was centered around the shifting balance of risk opening the door to a Sept cut. It all hinges on labor, as leading indicators point to a limited jump in tariffs-induced inflation thus far. Expectations remain anchored."

José Torres, senior economist at Interactive Brokers:

"Chair Powell’s acknowledgement that labor risks are skewed to the downside at this morning’s Jackson Hole speech is reigniting animal spirits in markets. The Fed Chief’s illustration of the societal pain of job losses and rising unemployment has fixed-income watchers penciling in two to three reductions by year-end while sparking investor enthusiasm amidst a near certainty of a quarter-point trim at next month’s meeting.

Indeed, participants understood those comments to mean that the FOMC is increasingly focused on employment weakness rather than price pressures at this juncture, even though Powell did allude to inflation uncertainties persisting."

Nick Timiraos, the Wall Street Journal's Fed watcher:

"Powell cautiously tees up a cut."

data.title: Real estate stocks outperform major market averages amid surging rate cut hopes
data.description:
It was a positive week for real estate stocks as interest rates took center stage.

The odds of a rate cut in September rose after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech at Jackson Hole.

At Jackson Hole, Powell remarked that a shifting balance of risks "may warrant adjusting our policy stance," signaling a potential willingness to lower the central bank’s policy rate if conditions justify it.

The chance of a September rate cut was at ~71% before the speech and rose to ~93% after it.

The rise in rate cut odds comes at a time when U.S. President Donald Trump has repeatedly criticized the Fed and Chair Powell for not lowering interest rates.

Meanwhile, Trump told aides that he's thinking about firing Fed Governor Lisa Cook if she doesn't resign after one of his housing officials accused her of mortgage fraud, the Wall Street Journal reported, citing a senior White House official and one other person.

If he fires Cook, Trump is poised to exert even more influence over the Fed's leadership.

The possible firing underscores the president's growing frustration with the central bank policymakers for not cutting interest rates.

If Trump is successful in removing Cook from the central bank's board of governors, he may be able to fill a fifth seat on the seven-member board.

And, assuming that Powell also steps down from the board in May, he'll get to nominate another board member.

Meanwhile, Cook has no intention to resign.

"I have no intention of being bullied to step down from my position because of some questions raised in a tweet," Cook said in an emailed statement to Bloomberg News via a Fed spokesperson. She said she's gathering accurate information to answer legitimate questions and provide facts.

  • Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

Nevertheless, after Powell's speech at the Jackson Hole Symposium, it is now widely expected that the Fed will slash its rates by a quarter point.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Index Sector (SP500-60) rose 2.42% from the prior week to close at 265.53, while the accompanying Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) added 2.44% to 42.34.

Comparatively, the benchmark S&P 500 index was up 0.27% only to 6,466.91.

Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) gained 2.76% to 2,696.02, while FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs closed 2.69% higher at 777.61.

Notable news

Home Depot's Q2 earnings indicate that the housing market has bottomed and is now turning a corner based on the late quarter improving trend.

Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) extended gains as market participants and influencers continued to tout the stock on social media.

Plymouth (PLYM) stock was surging after the industrial REIT announced the receipt of an unsolicited, non-binding acquisition proposal from Sixth Street Partners.

Eastgroup Properties (EGP) raised its quarterly dividend per share by 10.7% to $1.55 from $1.40.

Real estate stocks with over 10% returns this week

  • Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) (+58.04% W/W)
  • Plymouth Industrial REIT (PLYM) (+41.02% W/W)
  • Fathom Holdings (FTHM) (+22.95% W/W)
  • Generation Income Properties (GIPR) (+21.21% W/W)
  • Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHC) (+12.76% W/W)
  • Service Properties Trust (SVC) (+12.00% W/W)
  • FrontView REIT (FVR) (+11.40% W/W)
  • Orion Properties (ONL) (+11.16% W/W)
  • Strawberry Fields REIT (STRW) (+10.40% W/W)

Subsector performances

data.title: Bulls vs. Bears: Is Palantir ahead of a long-term downtrend?
data.description:
Shares of Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) fell ~10% this week as the enterprise software firm marked its longest losing streak in more than a year, generating sizable gains for investors betting against the company and drawing a range of views from Seeking Alpha analysts.

According to financial data provider S3 Partners, Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) shorts generated more than $1.6B in paper profits as the stock shed more than $73B in market capitalization during the slide, falling ~17% from its recent peak on Aug. 12.

With the Denver, Colorado-based firm continuing to trade nearly 400% higher than its year-ago levels, Seeking Alpha analysts debate the prospects of Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR).

The Bulls

“With PLTR stock down about 13% in the last five trading sessions, investors are undoubtedly nervous,” acknowledged Seeking Alpha analyst Rick Orford in Palantir: Why You'll Regret Selling Now (It's Not What You Think).  “Despite a recent dip on a short-seller report, Palantir’s fundamentals remain strong, with Q2 ‘25 revenue up 48% year-over-year and robust deal pipelines.”

“With a stock price of $177 per share, PLTR trades at a forward EV/Sales of nearly 100x and forward EV/EBITDA of 216x, far above the sector medians,” added Pythia Research in Palantir's Dual Engines Are Taking Off. “Despite trading near 250x forward P/FCF, investors pay a premium for PLTR's durability as dual-engine AI infrastructure.”

“PLTR's massive government and commercial contracts, expanding AI platforms, and strong execution justify its premium valuation and multi-year growth runway,” noted analyst Nauman Khan in Palantir: Buy The AI Goldmine (Upgrade). “Valuation is high, but with accelerating growth, cash generation, and product innovation, I see Palantir as a top AI play despite execution risks.”

“Q2 2025 was a big milestone for Palantir after finally surpassing for the first time the $1 billion mark in quarterly revenue,” opined SA analyst Deep Value Investing in Palantir: Rule Of 40 Breakout - A Double Edged Sword. “Near term, I'm watching Q3 (largest sequential revenue guide), the CFO's 91 full year Rule of 40 target, and the timing of Army EA bookings under the $10B agreement.”

The Bears

“Palantir's valuation is extremely stretched, driven by speculative trading,” noted analyst Bashar Issa in  Palantir: A Bubble-Burst Is Coming (Rating Downgrade). “I see PLTR stock as significantly overvalued and vulnerable to a correction.”

“Despite robust operational performance, Palantir's valuation is extremely stretched, making the stock unattractive even under aggressive future growth scenarios,” added Daniel Jones, Investing Group Leader of Crude Value Insights in Palantir Technologies: This Drop Is Only The Start Of What Is Deserved. “Even with optimistic growth and margin assumptions, Palantir would only approach fair value by 2028, highlighting significant downside risk at current prices.”

“The business itself is firing on all cylinders, posting a spectacular Q2 with record contract value, a massive guidance raise, and a stunning 94% score,” noted Oakoff Investments, Investing Group Leader of Beyond the Wall Investing in Palantir: It's Unlikely To End Well (Rating Downgrade). “I think the risk/reward for new buyers is terrible.”

“Palantir’s shares might be in a bubble territory, and the margin of safety for holding them is practically non-existent at this stage,” argued analyst Bears of Wall Street in Palantir: The Hype Has Reached Delusional Levels. “Considering this, there's a risk that if the macro environment once again worsens, the share price will once again collapse since trading at the current multiples is unsustainable in the long run.”

data.title: Insider trades: Procter & Gamble, Boeing, McDonald's among notable names this week
data.description:
This week witnessed insider transactions from companies like Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG), Boeing and McDonald’s. The following are purchases and sales made between August 18 and August 22.

  • Several Procter & Gamble (PG) executives shed their holdings in the company this week. Of them, the biggest transaction came from Alexandra Keith, CEO of beauty, who sold 11,463 shares at $158.16 each, worth about $1.81 million, leaving her with about 39,396 shares. Gary Coombe, CEO of grooming, disposed of 10,194 shares at the same price, generating roughly $1.61 million. His remaining holdings stand at 36,751 shares. PG’s CEO of fabric & home care, Sundar Raman, sold 9,554 shares at $158.16 apiece for about $1.51 million. He now owns about 50,252 shares in the firm.
  • At Home Depot (NYSE:HD), Hector Padilla, EVP, US sales and operations, sold 1,585 shares at $404.17, totaling nearly $640,609. He retains ownership of 13,929 shares.
  • McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) Executive Vice President and Chief Impact Officer Jonathan Banner sold 1,000 shares at $310 each, for $310,000. He holds about 99 shares in the fast-food chain post-sale.
  • Michael Huerta, director at Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL), sold 2,500 shares at $60.70, amounting to $151,762, and now owns 33,245 shares.
  • Twilio’s (NYSE:TWLO) CFO, Aidan Viggiano, let go of a total of 1,807 shares in the price range of $104.96 to $107.09, for a value of about $191,433. His stake in the firm now totals 146,016 shares.
  • On the buy side, Kelcy Warren, director at Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET), purchased 2 million shares at $17.36 apiece, worth roughly $34.68 million. Following the purchase, his ownership at the firm totals more than 372 million shares.
  • Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) Director Mortimer Buckley acquired 2,200 shares at $226.10 each, totaling about $497,420. His holding now stands at 2,200 shares.
  • At Under Armour (NYSE:UAA), Director Mohamed El-Erian bought 100,000 shares at $5.20, amounting to $519,960. His stake now totals about 284,108 shares.

data.title: Neuropsychiatric drug developer LB Pharma files for U.S. IPO
data.description:
LB Pharmaceuticals (LBRX), a New York-based drug developer focused on neuropsychiatric diseases, such as schizophrenia, has submitted regulatory filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission, seeking a listing on the Nasdaq Global Market.

According to an S-1 filing submitted to the SEC on Friday, the clinical-stage biopharma, whose lead candidate is a Phase 3-ready schizophrenia candidate known as LB-102, proposes to list on Nasdaq under the ticker symbol LBRX.

LB Pharmaceuticals (LBRX), launched in 2015, had an accumulated deficit of roughly $114.5M as of June 30, as its losses expanded from $6.3M in 2023 to $63.1M in 2024 amid rising expenses related to the development of its pipeline.

With no revenue from product sales, it signed several Royalty Participation Agreements with some of its existing investors in 2016 to sell up to 5% of royalties linked to LB-102 in exchange for $0.2M of upfront cash.

As of June 30, 2025, LBRX recorded cash and cash equivalents of $14.2M, which it said could be sufficient to fund operations and capital expenditures for at least 12 more months.

data.title: CIFR, AHL, BBAR, CRCL move the most in week's financials roundup
data.description:
Financial stocks rose markedly this week with the overall equity market as the Federal Reserve moved towards a long-awaited dovish stance.

With Fed Chair Jerome Powell opening the door to an interest-rate cut next month, the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) climbed 2.1% in the week ended Aug. 22, matching the S&P 500 Index's rise.

Cipher Mining (CIFR), a crypto miner and data center operator, rose the most of any financial stock (with market cap over $2B) in the past week, surging 23%, as crypto prices also soared;

Aspen Insurance Holdings (AHL) jumped 22% after a report that Japan's Sompo Holdings is in talks to acquire the insurer;

Singapore-based online brokerage UP Fintech Holding (TIGR) gained 17%;

Riot Platforms (RIOT), another bitcoin (BTC-USD) miner, gapped up 16.7%; and

Regional lender Bancorp (TBBK) rounded out the five biggest winners with a 10% advance.

On the losing side, Argentine bank BBVA Argentina (BBAR) dropped 13% on the back of its second-quarter results; 

Stablecoin issuer Circle Internet Group (CRCL), which during the week said it acquired blockchain infrastructure firm Informal Systems' high-performance consensus engine, Malachite to power its new Arc blockchain; retreated 9.5%;

Ether (ETH-USD) treasury company BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR), which during the week disclosed another weekly ETH purchase, shed 7.5%;

Brazil's XP (XP) slid 6.3% after turning in Q2 earnings; and

Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL), a fellow Argentine lender, drifted down 6.2%.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Caterpillar, Palo Alto and HPE among top picks
data.description:
The S&P500 (SP500) closed in the green on Friday, after investors this week digested headlines on retail earnings, more tariff-related news from the Trump administration, and Fed Chair Jerome Powell's closely watched speech.

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) lost over 2%, while Dow (DJI) fell 0.3%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Caterpillar upgraded on improving margin, discounting outlook

Evercore lifted the rating for Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) to Outperform from In-line, citing improving margin dynamics and signs of reduced discounting.

Analyst David Raso highlighted Caterpillar’s ability to withstand pricing pressures, writing that its "recent resilience in construction equip margins amid price-cost negativity a reminder of the power of volume leverage and geographic mix."

The brokerage set a price target of $476 on the stock.

Palo Alto upgraded after quarterly results

Bank of America upgraded Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW) to Buy from Hold after it reported better-than-expected quarterly results and guidance.

BofA analyst Tal Liani said the results and guidance were “impressive” on all fronts.

“With the stock down about 15% from the date of CyberArk M&A announcement, and given the solid fundamentals, we are upgrading from Neutral to Buy and maintaining our $215 PO, suggesting 22% upside potential to the stock,” BofA said.

Wells Fargo analyst Andrew Nowinski has an Overweight rating and $235 PT on Palo Alto.

HPE upgraded on Juniper acquisition

Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) was upgraded to Overweight from Neutral by Morgan Stanley due to its Juniper acquisition as enterprise hardware and storage stocks benefit from artificial intelligence demand.

The brokerage also increased its price target to $28 from $22 and said with the closure of Juniper, it sees 18% upside to FY26 consensus EPS, with EPS growing to $2.70-3.00 in FY27. It believes that as the market comes to better understand nearly half of HPE's business is networking, inclusive of more AI exposure, HPE's multiple will re-rate above the current 8x multiple.

MS also reiterated its Overweight rating on Dell (NYSE:DELL) and increased PT to $144 from $135.

UBS upgraded CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) to Buy from Hold saying that the company has a compelling valuation and good growth prospects. The firm, which raised PT to $79 from $67, said that CVS has executed well over the past two quarters and there are "early signs that the Healthcare Benefits (HCB) segment fixes are on track."

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) was in focus after KeyBanc Capital Markets raised PT on the Jensen Huang-led company to $215 from $190, with analyst John Vinh saying that he expects Nvidia to report strong Q2 results and guide Q3 slightly below consensus. Similarly, Susquehanna analyst Christopher Rolland also upped PT to $210 from $180 on the stock.

Ladenburg Thalmann double downgraded Nano Nuclear Energy (NASDAQ:NNE) to Sell from Buy with a $9 PT, citing higher expected cost, significant dilution risk, and increasing competitive pressure. Ladenburg analyst Michael Legg said he had hoped Nano's management would streamline its strategy and prioritize core initiatives, particularly the Kronos reactor, during its Q3 business update; instead, the company "continues promoting a broad, diversified model encompassing ancillary ventures in fuel, transportation, and consulting," which are "distractions" for a company of Nano’s size and capitalization.

KGI Securities downgraded Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) to Neutral from Outperform and lowered its PT to $75 from $95. The analysts expressed concern about potential competition in the custom application-specific integrated circuit market, which could negatively impact Marvell's business.

data.title: Meta in pact with Midjourney to license AI image and video models
data.description:
Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) has signed a partnership with Midjourney to license the startup’s AI image and video generation technology for the social media giant’s future products as part of a collaboration between the companies’ R&D teams.

“We are incredibly impressed by Midjourney. They have accomplished true feats of technical and aesthetic excellence, and we are thrilled to be working more closely with them,” said Meta Chief AI Officer Alexandr Wang in a post on Threads late Friday.

“This technical collaboration between our research teams is part of our effort to team up with the best companies in the industry whose work and expertise complements our own,” he added.

The financial terms of the collaboration were not available. However, Midjourney’s CEO David Holz said in an X post on Friday that his company “remains an independent, community-backed research lab, with no investors.”

According to TechCrunch, the Facebook owner could leverage the Midjourney deal to take on leading AI image and video models, such as Sora, developed by Microsoft (MSFT)-backed OpenAI, and Google’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Veo.

The partnership comes at a time when Meta’s (NASDAQ:META) AI efforts have come under public scrutiny. Earlier this week, The New York Times reported that the company is reorganizing its AI division, "Meta Superintelligence Labs," amid internal tensions.
